title: Lindy Effect

content.statement: The longer something has survived, the longer its expected remaining life.
content.question: Among my current bets, which are Lindy-proof and which are trend-dependent?
content.equation: Expected Remaining Life ≈ Time Already Survived
content.mental_model: Old book vs a new one, survival predicts survival.
content.business_lens: Bet on durable foundations (proven business models, time-tested principles) before chasing trends.
content.life_lens: Choose books, habits, and relationships that have already endured; survival is signal.
sop: List candidate ideas, technologies, or strategies
Estimate how long each has survived
Weight bets toward those with longer track records
Treat new things as higher-risk experiments
content.explainer: Mathematics, double-entry bookkeeping, and most physical laws are Lindy-strong. SaaS pricing models, social platforms, and management fads are not. Lindy thinking biases toward durable foundations without ruling out experimental bets.
questions: Among my current bets, which are Lindy-proof and which are trend-dependent?
What in your business model has survived unchanged for years, and what have you rebuilt three times already?
If you had to bet your next year of effort on either a proven, boring method or a trending one, which has actually paid off for you before?
content.equation_example: A tactic or business model that has survived 8 years has an expected remaining life of roughly 8 more years, under this logic. A tool that's 6 months old has an expected remaining life of roughly 6 more months. Longer survival so far means a longer expected future; a short track record means treat it as an experiment, not a foundation.
content.closing_statement: What survived long already tends to keep surviving.

caption:
Principle Nº 321: Lindy Effect

The longer something has survived, the longer its expected remaining life.

Ask yourself: Among my current bets, which are Lindy-proof and which are trend-dependent?
